title: Tell me about yourself — walk me through your background and resume.

transcript.text: Tell me about yourself — walk me through your background and resume.

transcript.thinking:
The user is asking Anshul to give a "tell me about yourself" / "walk me through your resume" answer. This is a classic opening interview question and one that should NOT use the STAR template.

Per the output format instructions:
- "Tell me about yourself" / "walk me through your resume": do NOT use STAR. Give a crisp spoken career arc — present (who I am now) → the 2–3 most relevant projects/impact → why I'm excited about this role — as a 🎙️ Say-it script of ~150 words, plus a one-line 💬 even-shorter version.

I need to:
1. Establish his current role and seniority level
2. Walk backward through his career arc: Walmart (SDE-III, June 2024–present) → Good Creator Co. (SDE-I, Feb 2023–May 2024) → PayU (intern → SDE-I, Jan 2022–May 2024)
3. Highlight 2–3 high-impact projects that show his main strengths
4. Keep the voice natural and spoken (like he's talking to a peer)
5. Make it ~150 words for the full script, plus a short 60-sec version
6. Use real numbers and real stack from the story bank

Let me construct this:

**Current role:** Walmart, SE-III, ~1 year, supplier-facing data APIs, real-time data venture
**Prior role:** GCC, 15 months, sole architect of 6 production services, ClickHouse migration hero
**First role:** PayU, 18 months, loan disbursal, went from intern to SE-I

Key projects to hit:
- ClickHouse migration (G1) — owned end-to-end, real scale
- Audit logging library (W2) — platform impact, 12+ teams
- Spring Boot 3 migration (W5) — technical rigor, zero downtime
- Keep the framing positive and forward-looking, focusing on what I built and learned rather than any friction. Frame the narrative around growth and technical ownership, ending on momentum and excitement for what's next.

blocks.md:
## Tell me about yourself — career arc intro

**Budget:** ~90 seconds spoken · **Framing:** general technical introduction, no specific company lens assumed

---

🎙️ **Say-it script** (what you'd say out loud):

> So I'm a backend engineer with about three and a half years of professional experience, mostly building high-throughput systems at scale. Right now I'm at Walmart as a Senior Engineer on the data-ventures team — that's supplier-facing APIs for customers like Pepsi and Coca-Cola. We handle real-time inventory queries, notifications, audit logging across multiple regions. Stack is Spring Boot, Java, Kafka, BigQuery, Postgres. I've been here about a year.
>
> Before Walmart, I spent about fifteen months at a smaller company called Good Creator Co. That was probably my highest-ownership role — I was the sole architect for six production services on a five-person team. Python and Go, ClickHouse, Airflow. The project I'm most proud of there is a ClickHouse migration where we moved off Postgres for analytics. Went from 30-second queries down to 12 seconds, cut storage costs 30%, zero data loss in the cutoff. That taught me a lot about making trade-offs when you're under constraint.
>
> My first job was at PayU in loan disbursal — started as an intern, ended as a software engineer. Did a lot of plumbing work there: idempotency, circuit breakers, test coverage from 30% to 83%. Real customer money, 24/7 on-call, first time shipping something that actually mattered.
>
> So that's the arc — intern doing scrappy operational fixes, sole architect running six services solo, now shipping platform-level infrastructure at a big company. Pretty happy with that trajectory.

---

💬 **60-second cut** (if they're crunched):

> Backend engineer, three and a half years — currently SDE-III at Walmart on supplier-facing data APIs. Before that, Good Creator Co., where I was sole architect of six services; shipped a ClickHouse migration that cut analytics latency 2.5x and costs 30%. Started as an intern at PayU doing loan-disbursal backend. I'm interested in [*company name*] because [*relevant value here*].

---

**What NOT to say:**
- ❌ Don't oversell: "world-class", "passionate about", or other generic fluff.
- ❌ Don't bad-mouth Walmart or any employer.
- ❌ Don't recite your resume as a list ("I have 3 years... I worked on X, Y, Z...") — tell it as a story.
- ❌ Don't end hanging ("...and that's about it") — tie it back to the role/company you're interviewing for.

---

**Likely follow-ups:**
- "Why are you leaving Walmart?" → *Positive framing only:* "I want to operate at a bigger scale and take on SDE-3 scope." Never complaints.
- "Tell me more about the ClickHouse migration." → G1 story, full STAR.
- "Which project are you most proud of?" → G1, full depth.
- "What's your biggest technical strength?" → Draw from W2 (platform/reusable-code thinking) or G1 (end-to-end ownership).
- "Why this company?" → Wait for them to name it, then tailor the why.

---

**Next move:** Once they name a specific question or company, I'll generate a full STAR answer or shift the company lens. What's the next question?
